author: @jeffreyanderson9719
text: The important parts involve the clean out door  and your welds. You have to make sure that it is 100 % air tight . Any air mixture that would get in would cause an explosion and of course you have the carbon monoxide,  and carbon dioxide  to watch for . The imbert design has a greater efficiency,  but the fema one will work with a bit of fiddling.  The sealant you use must be of a high enough heat resistance( the fema ones run cooler ) . The safest method of the clean out door would be a bolt down method with a flange that connects to the beer keg and sealantbetween that . Hope that helps.

author: @michaelpearson5465
text:
Interstingly, the idea of gasification is in fact incorperated into conventional powerplants, be that biomass, natural gas or even coal.

The exhaust gasses from these processes are not immediatly released from the exhuast. A proportion of the exhaust gasses are fed back into the furnace. There are two main advantages to this. Firstly, it increases the efficency of the power plant and secondly, by feeding the exhaust gasses back into the furnace you produce a "cleaner" burn. Reducing the amount of carbin manoxide and other harmful chemicals that are ejected.

The final stage for the exhuast gasses that do leave the furnace, travel through devices called scrubbers which filter out as much of the particulates and harmful gasses still prestent.

Power stations a required to provide real time data, to regulate the amout of pollutants emmited. So this process is incredibly important to achieve lower emmisions.

author: @tdtrecordsmusic
text: I weld. Took all the courses possible in my local college. Welded for work and for hobby.    U mentioned ur just gonna give it a go ... umm .. hold on there.    First grab scrap which is similar, get your technique down, then do the job correctly and it is dead simple.   Take any skilled welder, put them on a foreign machine and the first thing they are going to do is strike arcs and figure out "what is going on" .  Don't touch the job piece until your beads are correct on the scrap piece.     Different thicknesses, Different machines, even different environments, all require different knob twists.  Just get some scrap metal with same thickness as your job pieces and get your settings right. Keep that scrap, caz when u change position ur probably going to sanity check on that scrap piece b4 hitting the job piece.   With arc, you gotta be aware that vertical(like welding on a wall) is very different than horizontal(flat on the table). Horizontal is easy.       btw, the smell of some electrode flux is really cool :)  Preheating=Golden

author: @alt-swe5503
text: That design will not get the heat to crack the tar, so you need a really good filter to save the generator. A heart ring (constricted flow where the heat intensifies) in the pipe is a small price to pay to improve that a lot. It could also be tricky to avoid the fire to burn up all the way  thru the feeding column, my guess is that you'll end up feeding the air from the side when all is said and done. Love the project. Good luck.

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text:
I wish you the best of luck but this gasifier is about to school you. First is the oxidation and pyrolysis zones are not fixed with a fema unit. This will result in the oscillation of the amount of gases produced at these layers and these layers will be constantly moving. (more fuel below your oxidation zone = more fuel that is oxidized > the more pyrolysis gas is created and then just the opposite) This is especially the case as you agitate the grate and flow the fuel through it. The pyrolysis gas is the resulting gases produced by the oxidizing the fuel. This is the raw gas with complex molecule chains; this gas is full of tar. The fema units do not use a heat focal point to thermally crack those chains further; thus resulting in higher tar content. Now back to the oscillation of the layers of your process, if there is too much tar in the raw gases it can overwhelm the reduction process if it is not large enough and or hot enough; resulting in excessive tar production and clogging up this process. The reduction process is endothermic and the physics of this is something that is fighting you. You need that reduction process as hot as you can get it. 1800*f and greater for engine grade gas. 

Now an Imbert gasifier uses a fixed oxidation process with a restriction just bellow this. This restriction is a focal point to force all produced heat and gas together through a small cross section to crack those complex molecule chains before entering the carbon / reduction bed. The simpler these chains the better. The reduction process is simply stripping the Oxygen out of those chains and recombining with carbon. ( This is how you are getting H2 CO and CH4.  An efficient reduction process will show a huge temperature differential from the point of oxidation to the point where the gas exits your grate. 

Tar TAR TAR!! Ok first a gasifier is a refinery and when tar is produced generally you are going to produce many different grades of it. Some grades will drop at higher due point and others will not. This is the deception ""See my filter is working, just look at all the tar it collected"" This is just one grade of it that dropped there and most likely this is only a small portion of what was actually produced. Your gasifier will be under heavy vacuum and how do we boil things at lower temperature? You place the process under vacuum. If you are producing any amount of tar, the odds are you are producing a grade that will stay in gas suspension bypassing any filter you put in front of it. Generally it will drop at the throttle plate of the engine. 

Your filter should be built to filter one thing and that is the dust. Creosote you simply condense and is as simple as cooling the gas and it will drop. You actually want some moisture in the gas to help with valve seats as they are hardened. Valve seat cracking is a common problem with gaseous fueled engines. 

I hope you take me up on building a charcoal unit. They are much easier than what you going to attempt, plus you can crack water directly. The gas output is stable with no intervention needed by the user. Just get it running, set it and forget it. We just built a charcoal kiln to heat the shop, it makes engine grade charcoal as it heats the shop. That fuel is basically free as we need to heat the shop anyways.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text:
If it were me knowing what I know. I would take that fire tube and make a stupid simple charcoal gasifier out of it. It needs a bottom, a single air jet leading in from the side at the bottom. Then you your top needs a sealed lid, (simple pipe fitting and removable cap) Just use a large enough one so you can service it inside. Then your gas exit port is located at the topside. A simple bucket with an in and out filled with some sort of media to get the dust out is all that is needed.

Direct water cracking you will find amazing!! This is as simple as dripping water into the air jet of this charcoal gasifier. It will flash to steam and get ripped apart with the Oxygen combining with the carbon and the H2 is then left freely. This is the crude method, you can take it further by using engine exhaust to flash steam and then streaming it into the unit. The better you can do this the more water you can crack. It boils down to how hot you can keep the reduction process. Too much water it will become too cold and reverse the process saturating the fuel with moisture. That said this is an excellent way to control the internal heat processes of a charcoal gasifier as these system generally run too hot and melt the air inlet jet.

author: @senorjp21
text: Biomass gasification is "energetically challenged" to quote Jim Mason.  He developed the GEK gasifier which includes 2 heat exchangers: 1 between the engine exhaust and the fuel, and 1 between the hot gas and the combustion air.  Imagine trying to dry and burn material above when the airflow is going down...  This design tends to need kiln-dried wood to work, and works better on charcoal.  Low temperatures tend to produce dirty gas.

author: @richardsandwell2285
text:
A few little tips, one is to flood the barrel with Pure Argon if you have any prior to welding, this creates a gas backing makes welding easier and reduces oxidation and porosity on the backside of the weld. It would have been nice if I could have given you a piece of stainless pipe and some 316L electrodes to keep the metals the same or similar, when welding steel to stainless I think the rods you use ideally will be 309MOL I believe this reduces the risk of dilution and cracking. 
Something to remember when welding stainless is a low current is usually the order of the day, especially on thin-walled beverage containers, Stainless is a poor conductor of heat, thus unlike steel heat rapidly builds up in one place causing a blow through. A series of short two-inch long runs and then move to the opposite side and repeat until all the runs join together is often best. With Stainless welding overheating of the metal can cause Carbon displacement in the heat-affected zones, leading to rusting.  Health & Safety with Stainless I was once told smoking CAN cause lung cancer but Stainless welding and Hexavalent Chromium definitely cause lung cancer, also wear goggles after the welding process, as the stainless cools and contracts about a third more than ordinary steel the slag from MMA welding often self-releases at quite a speed usually heading towards your eyeballs. 
Cutting this kind of stainless can be done with a Jigsaw on a very slow speed with Tallow lube, it is the same with drilling, slowly does it or the tip of the drill is instantly blunt, those very thin 1mm thick cutting disks for small grinders help, but I have also used a 316 electrode with the welder on full power to pierce and cut out circles, a Carbon rod might also do this.
Most welding projects I do these days are in Stainless, it is the cheapest option, I tend to buy the Stainless from a friendly Scrapyard.  It just saves so much time and money having to paint everything, and with combustion projects like this, it lasts virtually forever compared to steel which just rusts and flakes to bits in no time.
Maybe practice on a bit of scrap stainless first, one technique I have used on thin stainless I call dragging and that is where you deposit quite a thin weld bead by increasing the speed at which you pass the rod over the surface, this reduces the heat gathering in one place, but takes some practice to achieve neat results.
Remember for most stainless builds that do not involve things being gas-tight, small one or two-inch runs usually suffice because the welds are many times stronger than steel welding, this saves electricity.  
If you have quite a thick flange to weld on, play the heat mainly on the thick part merely allowing the arc pool to simply run over and burn into the thinner metal. Good Luck, I may have welded the odd beverage container myself in my time lol. It is naughty and some breweries moan and groan about it, but speak to any pub landlord and they will tell you it is the breweries who never bother to pick the damn things back up from the pubs, many pubs are overrun with them.

author: @tkmotors991
text: Excellent I ran my Subaru Leone on a syngas side draft gasifier I built it was great apart from all the carbon and clogged up the valves eventually I came to the conclusion of an old air conditioning idea they used in the old days for big buildings basically a tunnel with water spraying into it cooling the gas down drying the gas out and cleaning it from carbon all problems solved in one compact arrangement I used a 6” flame tube for around 70 hp 🤙😁

author: @BensWorkshop
text: I'd be inclined to use a steel outer, possibly an old propane tank.

replies.author: @michaelpearson5465
replies.text: Bit overkill to be honest, the system wont be under a lot of preseure and beer barrels are designed to withdtand high pressures themselves, although relatively thin compared to a propane bottle, the ribs which are pressed into the material actually make the cylinder much stronger by increasing the effective depth of the material. You can see the same effect in a baked bean tin. They are extremly thin and can withstand high pressures caused by the heating of the content to sterilise the content.

replies.author: @BensWorkshop
replies.text: @michaelpearson5465 I'm more concerned about the heat.

replies.author: @michaelpearson5465
replies.text: @BensWorkshop stainless steel melts at over 1500°c the max temp a wood fire of this size would reach is 600°c so not even close to the melting point of stainless steel, also consider that the stainless steel jacket is a fair distance from the heat source meaning the heat from the fire will be much lower by the time it has transfered to the stainless steel. At most it may warp slightly but not by a noticable ampunt and this would not cause any issues.

replies.author: @BensWorkshop
replies.text: @michaelpearson5465 Kegs are made of aluminium.

replies.author: @BensWorkshop
replies.text: Or at least, most of them are, so that they are light.

replies.author: @idrisddraig2
replies.text: I make skis, and my prototype press for years was a vacuum setup - Fridge motors!, Slow yes, but depth of vaccum to rival any lab pump. If you want speed on your initial vaccum pull down use a cheap AC service pump, or even a shop vac!
